frameworks.name: Unit Economics as North Star for Franchising
frameworks.definition: Every franchise-demand problem traces back to a single root cause: the unit economics of each individual restaurant. Fix per-location profitability first; franchise interest is a downstream symptom.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that every problem in restaurant franchising can be solved by fixing one core metric: unit economics. If each restaurant location has compelling per-unit profitability, franchise demand follows without active selling; weak franchise sales are a symptom of weak per-location economics, not a sales or marketing problem.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.mechanism: Prospective franchisees evaluate expected return on investment; if unit economics are strong (e.g., low opening cost relative to first-year profit), the offer is self-evidently compelling and sells itself. Weak unit economics make the offer unattractive regardless of sales effort.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that whatever is making a restaurant unattractive for the founder to scale as a corporate location is the same factor making it unattractive for franchisees to buy in. The two problems — slow corporate expansion and slow franchise sales — share a single root cause: insufficient per-unit profitability.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: restaurant group with both corporate and franchise locations
claims.mechanism: Corporate expansion is constrained by the same returns that would discourage a franchisee; low per-unit profitability makes the investment unattractive from both the owner's and the buyer's perspective.

claims.text: Alex Hormozi illustrates that a restaurant franchise business generating $100 million in system-wide revenue at a 7% royalty rate, running 40% EBITDA at the franchisor level, and sold at a 12x multiple yields approximately $20-23 million to the founder after a typical 30%+ rollover requirement. He presents this as a relatively modest outcome for achieving $100M system-wide revenue.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi

numbers.axis: founder exit proceeds from $100M system-wide franchise revenue

bounded_by: Assumes 7% royalty rate; rates vary by franchise system
Assumes 12x multiple; aggressive franchise brands may command 15x

claims.text: Alex Hormozi argues that for a four-location restaurant group doing $9M in revenue with $1.2M in profit, growing to 20 corporate locations and improving per-unit profitability to $500K each would produce $10M in annual profit and an $80M exit at 8x — a significantly better outcome than pursuing 100 franchise units to reach $100M system-wide revenue.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: restaurant group currently at 4 corporate locations
ability to improve per-unit profitability to ~$500K

numbers.axis: exit proceeds: corporate scale vs. franchise scale

anchors.region: corporate scale path: 20 locations at $500K each
franchise scale path: 100 franchise units, $100M system-wide

bounded_by: Requires ability to improve per-unit profitability
Corporate path assumes 8x multiple; multiple depends on growth rate and margins

claims.text: Alex Hormozi recommends that the restaurant owner spend 24 months focused entirely on improving per-location profitability before pursuing franchise growth, treating the economics optimization as the prerequisite to any franchise sales strategy.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: restaurant franchise with underperforming unit economics
claims.mechanism: Franchise demand is a lagging indicator of unit profitability; until per-unit returns are compelling, marketing and sales effort is wasted on an unattractive offer.

numbers.axis: time investment in economics optimization before franchise push

anchors.value: franchise sales become easier due to compelling unit economics

claims.text: Alex Hormozi uses Panda Express as a benchmark: if Panda Express can run 27% net margins with the overhead required to operate 3,000+ stores and all associated corporate infrastructure, a smaller independent restaurant operator should be able to achieve at least 27% net margins without that overhead burden.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
conditions: lower overhead than a 3,000-unit QSR chain
claims.mechanism: Smaller operators have lower fixed overhead than large chains; if a chain with massive corporate overhead achieves 27% net margin, a lean operator should achieve at least the same, making 27% a minimum viable benchmark, not a ceiling.

numbers.axis: net margin benchmark for restaurant profitability

anchors.region: Panda Express (3,000+ store chain, heavy corporate overhead)

bounded_by: Panda Express operates a standardized QSR model; artisan or slow-smoked BBQ concepts have different COGS profiles

claims.text: Alex Hormozi asserts that the most honest and effective franchise sales approach is to state verifiable facts about existing location economics rather than making promises about future performance, because franchise disclosure obligations (FDD) legally constrain what can be promised but do not prevent presenting actual historical results.
claims.speaker: Alex Hormozi
claims.speaker_term: state the facts and tell the truth
claims.mechanism: Prospective franchisees evaluate disclosed financial performance data from existing units; strong historical unit economics serve as the proof that motivates purchase without requiring seller promises.
bounded_by: Only applicable when existing franchise units have sufficiently strong economics to present

examples.text: A restaurant franchise owner with four corporate locations doing $9M in revenue and $1.2M in profit, plus one open franchise unit and two more signed, approached Alex Hormozi for advice on selling more franchises. He believed franchises could not be actively sold because the purchase required a lifestyle change, and therefore buyers had to self-select. Hormozi rejected the passive framing and reoriented the conversation: the difficulty selling franchises was not a sales methodology problem but a unit economics problem. The franchisee offer was not compelling enough, and improving per-unit profitability — not marketing or sales tactics — was the required first step.

practices.text: When pitching franchise opportunities, present actual financial data from existing open locations (as disclosed in the FDD) rather than making forward-looking promises. Let the unit economics speak for themselves.

Before investing in franchise sales and marketing, spend dedicated time (Hormozi suggests ~24 months) optimizing per-location profitability down to the penny. Only pursue franchise growth once unit economics are genuinely compelling.

When evaluating a franchise versus corporate expansion strategy, model both paths fully to exit proceeds — including royalty rate, franchisor EBITDA margins, exit multiple, and rollover requirement — before committing to the franchise path.
